items.why: Students use English -s plural. German has multiple plural patterns including UMLAUT + er for many masculine nouns: Mann to Männer, Buch to Bücher, Land to Länder. Plurals must often be memorised.

items.q: When does 'in' take the accusative vs dative?
items.correct: Accusative for movement INTO a place; dative for location IN a place.
items.misconception: It always takes the same case.
items.random_wrong: It depends on the speaker's mood.
items.why: Students don't realise two-way prepositions exist. Wechselpräpositionen change case by meaning: 'Ich gehe in die Stadt' (motion, accusative); 'Ich bin in der Stadt' (location, dative).
Students assume gender follows meaning. German gender is grammatical, not biological. The diminutive suffix -CHEN makes any noun neuter, regardless of natural gender. So das Mädchen, das Mädel.
The answer examiners want is: Mittwoch. “Donnerstag” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Yellow. “Green” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many days are in a week in German vocab (Montag bis Sonntag)?
items.why: The real figure is 7. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
